title: The Dungeon Dive's Top 50 Games of All Time* - part 2
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c3rRnSi93Lw

The middle stretch from Daniel (@dungeondive)'s top-50 — games 40 down to 31 — leans heavily into the rules-heavy and the wildly ambitious.

**Dungeon Universalis** opens the run as the closest a boxed crawl has come to a true RPG, and probably plays best as one — one-vs-many over pure co-op. **Etherfields** lands at 39 for its Lynchian dream-logic and the rare sense that the game is *about* something, undercut only by how brutally hard it is to pick back up after a break. **Folklore: The Affliction** at 38 needs Daniel's house rules and the Spire expansion's initiative track, but the dark fairy-tale setting earns its place. **Dungeon Run** (37) is the begging-for-an-expansion semi-co-op battle royale; **Legacy of Dragonholt** (36) is Nikki Valens at the top of her game-book design.

Lower down: **Tales of the Arabian Nights** (the epitome of "anything can happen, including becoming a monkey"), **Prophecy** (Talisman with more choices), **Space Hulk 4th Edition** (a top-five-produced game, hampered only by being two-player), **Mini Rogue** (newest entry, lowest-effort/highest-payoff on the list), and **Space Cadets: Away Missions** with its overkill-dice combat that Daniel wishes more designers would steal.

----

Daniel argues game designers should think more like hip-hop artists or vaporwave producers — lifting the *good* mechanisms from each other and leaving the bad ones behind. Which mechanism in this batch deserves to be plundered into more games?
